name: cable incline fly (on stability ball)
instructions: Set up a stability ball at an incline angle.
Attach the cable handles to the high pulleys of a cable machine.
Sit on the stability ball facing away from the machine, with your feet firmly planted on the ground.
Grasp the cable handles with an overhand grip, palms facing forward.
Lean forward slightly, keeping your back straight and core engaged.
With a controlled motion, bring your arms out to the sides, keeping a slight bend in your elbows.
Continue the motion until your arms are parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment, then slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Set up a stability ball at an incline angle. Attach the cable handles to the high pulleys of a cable machine. Sit on the stability ball facing away from the machine, with your feet firmly planted on the ground. Grasp the cable handles with an overhand grip, palms facing forward. Lean forward slightly, keeping your back straight and core engaged. With a controlled motion, bring your arms out to the sides, keeping a slight bend in your elbows. Continue the motion until your arms are parallel to the ground. Pause for a moment, then slowly return to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
